title: Chicken and dumplings
subtitle: Slow-cooked chicken in a thick broth with soft dropped dumplings
excerpt: A stew of pulled chicken in a rich, creamy broth with carrots, celery, and onion, topped with soft, cloud-like dropped dumplings that steam on top of the stew. The dumplings are a simple baking-powder dough spooned directly onto the simmering liquid — they puff in the steam and cook through in about 15 minutes.
sourceNotes: Chicken and dumplings is a staple of Southern and Midwestern American cooking with roots in both British suet-dumpling traditions and the practical cooking of rural America. USDA Extension Service publications on Southern home cooking from the early twentieth century document the preparation. Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) includes dropped-dumpling preparations for stews.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The chicken stew base (without dumplings) can be made up to 2 days ahead. Reheat to a simmer before adding the dumpling dough.

content.text: Dropped dumplings are not the same as rolled suet dumplings. They are softer, lighter, and cooked by steam rather than by simmering in liquid. The dough is sticky and loose (too loose to roll) and is dropped by the spoonful onto the surface of a simmering stew. The lid goes on immediately and is not removed for 15 minutes; the steam trapped inside the pan does all the work. Removing the lid too early deflates the dumplings.

Use chicken thighs rather than breast meat; they stay tender through the initial cooking and pull apart into satisfyingly rough pieces. The broth is enriched with a little cream or crème fraîche at the end, after the dumplings have cooked, to give it the richness that makes the dish more than just a soup.

Brown the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs skin-side down in a little oil in a large saucepan over medium-high heat for 5 minutes. Flip and cook for 2 minutes more. Remove and set aside. Add the {{onion}}, {{carrot}}, {{celery}}, and {{garlic}} to the pan and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and return the chicken to the pan. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 25 minutes. Remove the chicken, strip the meat from the bones and skin, and return the pulled meat to the broth. Discard the bones and skin.

Rub the {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter into the {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour with your fingertips until it resembles coarse crumbs. Add a pinch of salt and the {{whole-milk}} of milk and stir with a fork until a soft, sticky dough forms. Do not overwork. Bring the stew back to a gentle simmer. Drop the dumpling dough in heaped tablespoons onto the surface of the stew, leaving space between them. They will expand. Put the lid on immediately and cook for 15 minutes without lifting the lid. The dumplings should be puffed and cooked through.
Stir the {{double-cream}} of double cream into the broth around the dumplings. Season with salt and pepper. Serve in deep bowls with dumplings on top.

items.cause: Dough overworked, or lid lifted during steaming
items.fix: Stir the dumpling dough only until it just comes together, and do not open the lid for the full 15 minutes

items.symptom: Dumplings sink into the stew rather than floating
items.cause: Stew not simmering when dumplings were added
items.fix: The liquid must be at a gentle simmer so the dumplings sit on the surface and steam; add them to cold or barely warm liquid and they sink and cook wrong

items.cause: Chicken stock was light, or not reduced
items.fix: Simmer the broth uncovered for 10 minutes after removing the chicken to reduce and concentrate it before adding the dumplings

items.symptom: Dumplings are wet and undercooked in the centre
items.cause: Not enough steam or too short a cooking time
items.fix: The 15-minute steam time is a minimum; insert a skewer into the largest dumpling — it should come out clean

content.text: Chicken and dumplings is a Southern and Midwestern American staple that draws on two traditions simultaneously: the British practice of adding suet or flour dumplings to broth-based stews, and the Southern practice of cooking chicken slowly in liquid until it falls from the bone. It is a farmhouse dish, built for economy and warmth, and it has remained in continuous, unaltered use in American domestic cooking because there is nothing in it that needs changing. It is one of those meals that defines comfort cooking in the most literal sense: warming, filling, and simple to make from ingredients that are always in the kitchen.
